AI has intensified this: training models requires significantly more power per rack than...

entries.title: Your Agent's Token Count Is a Vanity Metric
entries.preview: Token count tells you how much text moved through the model, not whether the agent did anything useful. A chatty agent that burns 50k tokens and solves the problem is worth more than a terse one that spends 5k and fails. Optimizing for fewer tokens quietly pushes you toward agents that do less,...

entries.title: Don't Trace Prompts, Trace the Tool Calls
You Don't Need an Eval Framework, Just 20 Examples
entries.preview: Teams reach for an eval framework before they have anything to evaluate, and spend a week wiring up harnesses, adapters, and dashboards. By the time the plumbing works, nobody remembers what question they were trying to answer. The framework becomes the project, and the actual quality of the agent...

entries.title: LLM-as-Judge Grades on a Curve
Guardrails Won't Save You; Least Privilege Will
entries.preview: Prompt-based guardrails ask the model to please not do the bad thing, and models are very good at being talked out of their instructions. Every guardrail is a filter with holes, and attackers only need to find one. Treating a system prompt as a security boundary is wishful thinking, not defense.

entries.title: Prompt Injection Has No Fix, Only Blast Radius
Hidden Traps in a Verifiable-Reward Environment
entries.preview: A verifiable reward sounds like the dream: instead of a fuzzy human judgment, you get a checker that says pass or fail with no argument. Unit tests, exact-match answers, and compilers give you a clean signal you can train against at scale. The promise is that you can finally optimize without a...

entries.title: Why Reward Hacking Still Beats Us
Vector DBs Are Not Needed for RAG
entries.preview: The moment someone says RAG, the team reaches for a dedicated vector database like it is a required checkbox. It is not. You are adding a whole new piece of infrastructure to store some embeddings before you have proven you even need approximate nearest neighbor search.

entries.title: Your Wiki RAG Fails at Chunking, Not the Model
Edison Didn't Invent the Lightbulb (and Knew It)
entries.preview: The story everyone learns is that Edison sat down, had a flash of genius, and handed the world electric light. That is not what happened. Incandescent lighting had dozens of people working on it for decades before Edison filed a single patent.

entries.title: Edison's Real Genius Was the Grid, Not the Bulb
Roman Roads Weren't Built for Trade
entries.preview: People picture Roman roads as ancient highways full of merchants hauling wine and grain across the empire. That image gets the purpose backwards. The roads were built by the state for the state, and commerce was a side effect, not the goal.

entries.title: Roman Concrete Heals Itself; Ours Just Crumbles
Index Funds for People Who Hate Spreadsheets
entries.preview: There is a myth that investing means tracking tickers, building budgets in cells, and rebalancing charts every weekend. You can skip all of it. A broad index fund is designed so that doing nothing is the strategy, not a compromise.

entries.title: How a Traditional IRA Quietly Loses You Money
Skip the Eurail Pass: Point Tickets Cost Less
entries.preview: A single flat rate for unlimited trains across a whole continent sounds like the obvious win. But the price is set for the traveler who rides constantly, and almost nobody actually rides that much. You end up paying for flexibility you never use.

entries.title: An Overnight Train Is a Hotel in Disguise
Machines Aren't Cheating: Against Barbell Purism
entries.preview: There is a loud camp that says free weights are the only real training and machines are for people who aren't serious. The claim is that barbells build stabilizers and machines let you cheat the hard part. It sounds tough, but it's mostly gym folklore.

entries.title: Soreness Is Not Progress
Skip the Raised Bed: a Bucket Grows More
entries.preview: Raised beds get sold as the beginner's starting point, but they are the expensive way in. By the time you buy lumber, hardware, and enough soil to fill a big box, you're hundreds of dollars deep before a single seed goes in. That cost stops a lot of people before they ever grow anything.

entries.title: Why Most Beginner Gardens Die in July
Skip the Telescope: Start With Binoculars
entries.preview: Almost everyone new to the night sky buys a telescope first, and most of those telescopes end up in a closet. They're fiddly to aim, they show a tiny patch of sky, and a cheap one delivers a blurry letdown. The scope isn't the on-ramp to astronomy, it's the thing that scares people off it.

entries.title: A Dark Sky Beats a Bigger Telescope
You Don't Need Immersion; You Need Output
entries.preview: Everyone tells you to move abroad or binge foreign films until fluency soaks in by osmosis. But immersion without effort is just background noise, and you can spend years surrounded by a language and still not speak it. Passive exposure feels productive because it is comfortable, and comfort is...

entries.title: Why Flashcards Stop Working at B1
Delete Your Meditation App
entries.preview: You downloaded a meditation app to escape your phone, and now your phone is where you go to calm down. The tool meant to quiet your mind has become one more thing pinging for your attention. Reaching for a screen to unplug is a contradiction most people never notice.

entries.title: Burnout Looks Like Cynicism, Not Exhaustion
Skip the Road Bike: a Used Hybrid Commutes Better
entries.preview: New cyclists walk into a shop and reflexively point at the fastest, lightest road bike on the wall. They picture themselves flying down open roads, not sitting at a red light with a backpack and a pannier. The road bike is built for a ride most commuters never actually take.

entries.title: Why Bike Lanes Pay for Themselves
Rooftop Solar Is the Wrong First Move
entries.preview: Homeowners who want to go green almost always start by pricing out rooftop panels. Solar feels like the big visible statement, the thing you can point at from the driveway. But leading with panels usually means paying to power a house that wastes half of what it takes in.
